pattern: Cyanotic infant with decreased pulmonary ﬂ ow

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a boot-shaped heart contour, characterized by an elevated cardiac apex and a concavity in the region of the main pulmonary artery segment. There is associated relative pulmonary oligemia indicating decreased pulmonary blood flow.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include a normal overall heart size and the potential presence of a right-sided aortic arch, which is seen in approximately 25% of cases. The skeletal structures and abdominal situs typically appear normal.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of gross cardiomegaly or prominent right atrial enlargement, which would suggest an alternative diagnosis such as Ebstein anomaly or pulmonary atresia with an intact ventricular septum. Furthermore, there is no evidence of pulmonary plethora to suggest transposition of the great arteries.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the classic combination of a boot-shaped heart with diminished pulmonary vascularity in a cyanotic neonate.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Tetralogy of Fallot, given the boot-shaped heart and decreased pulmonary vascularity. This is the most common cyanotic congenital heart disease, characterized by the tetrad of right ventricular outflow tract obstruction, ventricular septal defect, overriding aorta, and right ventricular hypertrophy.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Pulmonary atresia, however in the setting of an intact ventricular septum, this typically presents with massive cardiomegaly filling the chest. While pulmonary atresia with a ventricular septal defect can mimic these exact findings as a severe variant, the completely atretic pulmonary valve would usually require different immediate management with prostaglandin infusion.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Tricuspid atresia, given it also presents with cyanosis and decreased pulmonary vascularity, and misdiagnosing the underlying anatomy could severely compromise surgical planning. However, tricuspid atresia typically lacks the classic boot-shaped apex of right ventricular hypertrophy, presenting instead with left ventricular hypertrophy and right atrial enlargement.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately map the cardiovascular anatomy, particularly evaluating the right ventricular outflow tract and pulmonary artery branches to guide surgical repair.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Reviewing fetal echocardiography and antenatal ultrasound is useful to see if the diagnosis was established prior to birth and to ascertain baseline cardiac function.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated postnatal echocardiogram as the initial modality of choice, followed by a cardiac MRI or CT angiogram, as these modalities are superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex internal cardiac anatomy, measuring ventricular volumes, and identifying major aortopulmonary collateral arteries (MAPCAs).
management.mriSequences: I would specify ECG-gated steady-state free precession (SSFP) cine sequences to evaluate right ventricular function and confirm the ventricular septal defect, alongside 3D contrast-enhanced MR angiography to perfectly delineate the pulmonary artery architecture.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Cardiology and Cardiothoracic Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the severity of the pulmonary stenosis, the size of the central pulmonary arteries, and the presence of any collateral vessels to determine the timing of a palliative shunt versus complete surgical repair.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a boot-shaped heart contour, characterized by an elevated cardiac apex and a concavity in the region of the main pulmonary artery segment. There is associated relative pulmonary oligemia indicating decreased pulmonary blood flow. Supporting features include a normal overall heart size and the potential presence of a right-sided aortic arch, which is seen in approximately 25% of cases. The skeletal structures and abdominal situs typically appear normal. Importantly, there is no evidence of gross cardiomegaly or prominent right atrial enlargement, which would suggest an alternative diagnosis such as Ebstein anomaly or pulmonary atresia with an intact ventricular septum. Furthermore, there is no evidence of pulmonary plethora to suggest transposition of the great arteries. The feature that most influences my thinking is the classic combination of a boot-shaped heart with diminished pulmonary vascularity in a cyanotic neonate.

The appearances are most in keeping with Tetralogy of Fallot, given the boot-shaped heart and decreased pulmonary vascularity. This is the most common cyanotic congenital heart disease, characterized by the tetrad of right ventricular outflow tract obstruction, ventricular septal defect, overriding aorta, and right ventricular hypertrophy. The principle differential would be Pulmonary atresia, however in the setting of an intact ventricular septum, this typically presents with massive cardiomegaly filling the chest. While pulmonary atresia with a ventricular septal defect can mimic these exact findings as a severe variant, the completely atretic pulmonary valve would usually require different immediate management with prostaglandin infusion. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Tricuspid atresia, given it also presents with cyanosis and decreased pulmonary vascularity, and misdiagnosing the underlying anatomy could severely compromise surgical planning. However, tricuspid atresia typically lacks the classic boot-shaped apex of right ventricular hypertrophy, presenting instead with left ventricular hypertrophy and right atrial enlargement.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately map the cardiovascular anatomy, particularly evaluating the right ventricular outflow tract and pulmonary artery branches to guide surgical repair. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Reviewing fetal echocardiography and antenatal ultrasound is useful to see if the diagnosis was established prior to birth and to ascertain baseline cardiac function. I would recommend a dedicated postnatal echocardiogram as the initial modality of choice, followed by a cardiac MRI or CT angiogram, as these modalities are superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex internal cardiac anatomy, measuring ventricular volumes, and identifying major aortopulmonary collateral arteries (MAPCAs). I would specify ECG-gated steady-state free precession (SSFP) cine sequences to evaluate right ventricular function and confirm the ventricular septal defect, alongside 3D contrast-enhanced MR angiography to perfectly delineate the pulmonary artery architecture. This should go to the Paediatric Cardiology and Cardiothoracic Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the severity of the pulmonary stenosis, the size of the central pulmonary arteries, and the presence of any collateral vessels to determine the timing of a palliative shunt versus complete surgical repair.
